my-website.summary: A React (TypeScript) single-page application that handles all user interaction. Deployed to Azure Static Web Apps, which serves the static build and rewrites all navigation to index.html so client-side routing works on deep links. Autocomplete on the Trail Finder search bar is powered by the Google Maps JS SDK running entirely in the browser.
my-website.description: A React (TypeScript) single-page application that hosts all user-facing features. Deployed to Azure Static Web Apps with a rewrite rule that sends all navigation to index.html so client-side routing works on deep links.
features: Digits — daily number puzzle game. Uses hardcoded stub data in development (no API call needed)
Momentum Finder — live NBA game cards with momentum shift indicators
Trail Finder — city search with Google Maps autocomplete (browser-side), trail recommendations, weather, and AI-generated gear list
Ideas Board — EasyAuth sign-in, feature idea list and project management, one-click bot trigger
Learning Plan — Google OAuth sign-in, AI-generated personalised study plans, save and review history
Architecture reference — internal system documentation (not linked from the main nav)

architecture.overview: Create React App with TypeScript and client-side routing via react-router-dom. Each route maps to a top-level TSX + CSS pair. A shared NavBar component appears on every view. REACT_APP_* environment variables are injected at build time by GitHub Actions.
keyPoints: public/staticwebapp.config.json rewrites all routes to index.html — required for deep-link navigation on Azure SWA
Digits uses process.env.NODE_ENV === "development" to skip the API and use hardcoded stub data locally
Trail Finder autocomplete uses the Google Maps JS SDK entirely in the browser — no backend round-trip, 300ms debounce
Every PR to main gets an isolated SWA preview environment, torn down automatically when the PR closes
Cross-repo deploy: after each backend service deploys, it commits updated metadata to this repo, triggering a frontend rebuild so the Architecture page stays current

designDocs.title: ADR: Per-Idea Status Updates
designDocs.description: Adds a threaded updates log to each idea, backed by a new Azure Table Storage table and a detail slide-over panel in the frontend.

designDocs.title: ADR: Dashboard GitHub Actions & Expandable Cards
designDocs.description: Adds GitHub Actions last-run status to each service card, replaces the flat metrics table with inline accordion expansion.

digits.summary: Generates, stores, and serves daily Digits puzzles via Azure Table Storage. Each invocation writes a metrics row (duration, status) to a metrics table so the dashboard can track request volume. In local development the frontend bypasses this API and uses hardcoded stub data instead.
digits.description: Azure Functions app that generates, stores, and serves daily number puzzles. Each request is instrumented with metrics rows written to Table Storage so the dashboard can track volume and latency.
features: Generates number puzzles with configurable difficulty (easy, medium, hard)
Stores puzzles in Azure Table Storage partitioned by date
Serves the current day's puzzles via a single HTTP GET endpoint
Writes per-request metrics rows (duration, status, puzzle count) to a metrics table
Three difficulty tiers returned in a single response — frontend sorts easy → medium → hard

architecture.overview: Two Azure Function triggers: a timer trigger that generates the day's puzzles at midnight UTC and writes them to Table Storage, and an HTTP trigger that reads and returns them. Table Storage acts as both puzzle store and metrics sink.
keyPoints: Frontend skips this API in development — NODE_ENV=development activates hardcoded stub data
Metrics rows are picked up by dashboard-api via Azure Table Storage queries
Timer trigger uses a cron expression: "0 0 * * *" (midnight UTC daily)
Puzzles are keyed by ISO date string — no cleanup needed, old rows simply age out

dataFlow.label: Frontend checks NODE_ENV
sublines: development → hardcoded stub data (no API call)
production → call Digits API

dataFlow.label: Digits API (Azure Functions)
sublines: returns puzzles sorted by difficulty
writes metrics row to Table Storage

momentum-finder.summary: Analyses live NBA game data to surface momentum shifts. Runs as a Container App (FastAPI + Uvicorn on port 80, scales 0–10 replicas). Emits structured JSON logs on every request that flow into Log Analytics for the dashboard to query.
momentum-finder.description: FastAPI Container App that analyses live NBA game data to detect and surface momentum shifts. Scales automatically between 0 and 10 replicas and emits structured JSON logs for dashboard monitoring.
features: Fetches live NBA game scores and play-by-play data
Detects scoring runs and momentum shifts within a game
Returns structured game cards with team info, score, period, and momentum indicators
/health endpoint consumed by dashboard-api for uptime monitoring
Structured JSON request logs flow into shared Log Analytics workspace

architecture.overview: FastAPI on Uvicorn (port 80), containerised and deployed as an Azure Container App. Shares a Log Analytics workspace with trail-finder for unified observability. The app scales to zero replicas when idle.
keyPoints: Scales 0–10 replicas — expect a cold start (~5–10s) on first request after idle
Shared Log Analytics workspace with trail-finder; KQL queries in dashboard-api span both services
REACT_APP_API_BASE_URL must be set in the frontend build for this feature to work
Structured logging middleware attaches request_id, duration_ms, and status_code to every log line

dataFlow.label: momentum-finder Container App
sublines: fetches live NBA data
analyses scoring runs and momentum

dataFlow.label: Live game cards rendered — teams, score, momentum indicators

trail-finder.summary: Given a city, geocodes it, searches Google Places for nearby trails, fetches weather from Open-Meteo, pulls AllTrails snippets via Google Custom Search, then synthesizes a condition summary and gear list with Azure OpenAI. Results are cached in memory for 24 hours. Runs as a Container App with the same logging middleware as momentum-finder.
trail-finder.description: FastAPI Container App that generates personalised trail recommendations for a given city. Fans out across Google Places, weather, and search APIs, then synthesizes a condition summary and gear list with Azure OpenAI. Results are cached 24h per location.
features: City search with Google Maps JS SDK autocomplete — runs entirely in the browser, zero backend cost
Geocodes the city and searches Google Places for nearby hiking trails
Fetches weekend weather forecast from Open-Meteo (free, no API key)
Pulls AllTrails snippets via Google Custom Search for additional trail context
Azure OpenAI synthesizes a condition summary and gear list from all sources
In-memory result cache keyed by normalised city name — 24h TTL
/health endpoint for dashboard monitoring

architecture.overview: FastAPI app with a sequential pipeline of external API calls fanned out where possible. Azure OpenAI synthesizes the final output from all gathered context. The in-memory cache is a Python dict — cleared on container restart.
keyPoints: Autocomplete is browser-side (Google Maps JS SDK) — 300ms debounce, no backend request until search is submitted
24h cache TTL balances freshness with API cost; weather is the most volatile component
Identical structured JSON logging middleware to momentum-finder — same KQL queries work for both
Scales to zero when idle — same cold start caveat as momentum-finder
Google Places and Custom Search quotas are the primary cost driver; OpenAI is secondary

dataFlow.label: User types city → Google Maps JS SDK autocomplete (browser)
Geocode city → lat/lon
sublines: Google Places text search for nearby trails
Open-Meteo weekend weather forecast
Google Custom Search for AllTrails snippets

dataFlow.label: Azure OpenAI synthesizes condition summary + gear list

dashboard-api.summary: Single aggregator endpoint that fans out in parallel to health-check momentum-finder and trail-finder, query Log Analytics (KQL) for request counts, latency, and recent errors, query the Digits metrics Table Storage table, and fetch Azure Cost Management MTD spend. Uses a system-assigned managed identity — no stored credentials.
dashboard-api.description: Azure Functions app that fans out in parallel to health-check all services, query Log Analytics for request metrics, pull Digits Table Storage metrics, and fetch Azure Cost Management spend — returning everything in a single aggregated response.
features: Health checks for momentum-finder and trail-finder (HTTP /health)
Log Analytics KQL queries for request counts, average latency, and errors per service (24h window)
Digits metrics from Table Storage (request volume, error rate)
Azure Cost Management MTD spend breakdown — cached 1h due to API rate limits

GitHub Actions API integration for latest workflow run status and failed jobs (optional per app); prefers push/dispatch/release events over scheduled runs; skips empty results when GITHUB_TOKEN is unset or API returns nothing
CORS OPTIONS handler added for preflight requests (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, Allow-Headers: Authorization Content-Type)
ErrorRow objects now include error_type and status fields for enhanced debugging and analytics
All data returned in a single response — frontend polls every 60 seconds

architecture.overview: Single HTTP-triggered function that executes all sub-queries in parallel via asyncio.gather. Uses a system-assigned Managed Identity — no credentials stored in app settings. RBAC roles are assigned by the azure-infrastructure package.
keyPoints: Managed Identity holds Log Analytics Reader (scoped to the shared workspace) and Cost Management Reader (scoped to subscription)
No API keys in environment variables — all Azure SDK auth flows through Managed Identity
Cost Management responses cached for 1h in memory; all other queries are live
GitHub Actions API requires GITHUB_TOKEN env var; gracefully degrades if unset; skips empty results when no token or runs are available; prefers push/dispatch/release events over scheduled runs
Frontend dashboard polls /api/dashboard every 60 seconds
Parallel fan-out keeps response time under 3s even with 5+ downstream calls
CORS OPTIONS handler added to support browser preflight requests (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, Allow-Headers: Authorization Content-Type)
ErrorRow objects now include error_type and status fields, ensuring richer error context across health checks, metrics queries, and GitHub Actions logs

dataFlow.label: dashboard-api (Azure Functions) — parallel fan-out
sublines: Health: GET momentum-finder /health, trail-finder /health
Log Analytics KQL: request counts, latency, errors (24h)
Table Storage: digits metrics rows (24h)
Cost Management: MTD spend by service (1h cache)
GitHub Actions: latest workflow run status (optional per app)

dataFlow.label: Dashboard renders health pills, metrics tables, cost breakdown, GitHub Actions status

designDocs.title: ADR: Dashboard GitHub Actions & Expandable Cards
designDocs.description: Adds GitHub Actions last-run status to each service card, replaces the flat metrics table with inline accordion expansion.

ideas-api.summary: Stores and serves AI-generated feature ideas in Azure Table Storage. Accepts browser JWT tokens via EasyAuth for read access and an X-Ideas-Key machine write-key for automated writes. Exposes endpoints to list and create ideas, manage projects, and trigger the ideas-bot Container App Job. Projects now support a single repository string instead of a list.
ideas-api.description: Azure Functions app that stores and serves feature ideas in Table Storage. Supports two auth modes: browser JWT tokens via EasyAuth for read access, and an X-Ideas-Key machine write-key for automated writes. Exposes a trigger endpoint for the ideas-bot.
features: PATCH /api/projects/{id} — updates project metadata, including associated repository, supports both EasyAuth JWT authentication and X-Ideas-Key machine write-key for access
Supports bot-managed fields, including 'bot_status', 'bot_pr_url', 'bot_error', and the newly added 'bot_blocked_by' for cross-repo dependency tracking

architecture.overview: HTTP-triggered Azure Functions backed by Table Storage. EasyAuth handles browser auth transparently at the Azure layer — no auth code in the application. A separate machine key enables automation (ideas-bot, external scripts) to write ideas without user credentials.
keyPoints: PATCH /projects/{id} endpoint updates project metadata, including the 'repo' field stored as a single string in Table Storage, now supports machine key authentication alongside user authentication
Backward compatibility: existing entities with old 'repos' JSON list have their first entry promoted to the 'repo' field on read
New 'bot_blocked_by' field added to support tracking cross-repo dependencies, integrated into the bot-managed fields alongside 'bot_status', 'bot_pr_url', and 'bot_error'

dataFlow.label: Bot updates idea status
sublines: Updates 'bot_status' field in the ideas table
Adds 'bot_blocked_by' field for cross-repo dependency tracking

designDocs.title: ADR: Per-Idea Status Updates
designDocs.description: Adds a threaded updates log to each idea, backed by a new Azure Table Storage table and a detail slide-over panel in the frontend.

ideas-bot.summary: Container App Job triggered by ideas-api. Clones the target repository, runs an AI-driven tool-use loop to implement the requested feature, commits the result, and opens a draft pull request on GitHub. The job scales to zero between invocations.
ideas-bot.description: A containerised Python agent that autonomously implements feature ideas. Triggered on demand by ideas-api, it clones the target repo, runs a GPT-4o tool-use loop to read/write files and execute shell commands, commits the result, and opens a draft pull request.
features: App-level retry mechanism for GPT-4o API calls with up to 4 attempts and exponential backoff (60/120/300s) to handle sustained 429 rate limits
Sliding context window for GPT-4o tool-use loop now trims older exchanges beyond the last 14 to reduce token growth and further mitigate 429 risks
Decomposes complex ideas into 2–4 focused sub-tasks, each assigned to the correct project based on repo-specific work, reducing token usage and API load
Sub-tasks are now ordered and can be chained with hard dependencies, ensuring backend tasks complete before dependent frontend tasks
Reduced token caps for tool outputs and agent loops: bash output capped at 2500 chars, read_file capped at 4000 chars, and max_tokens reduced to 4096
Fetches CLAUDE.md from the target repository before the clarity check and agent execution, injecting repo-specific context to improve decision-making and reduce unnecessary questions
Prior Q&A from previous triggers is now fetched and injected into the clarity check, preventing repeated questions and improving efficiency

Added query_table tool for read-only access to Azure Table Storage, enabling the agent to inspect live ideas, projects, and updates tables to validate assumptions and understand record shapes before writing code
Increased agent round limit from 40 to 60, with a wrap-up nudge injected at round 50 to preserve partial work and ensure progress is committed before hitting the limit
Agent now surfaces a summary explanation when no commits are made, allowing users to understand why no changes were implemented and providing actionable feedback
Added support for posting to the Writing API (quixotry.me) via a subprocess helper function, enabling the bot to create posts headlessly using a pre-warmed token cache

architecture.overview: A containerised Python agent that autonomously implements feature ideas. Triggered on demand by ideas-api, it clones the target repo, performs a pre-flight clarity check, runs an AI-driven tool-use loop to read/write files and execute shell commands, commits the result, and opens a draft pull request.
keyPoints: GPT-4o tool-use loop now includes app-level retry mechanism with up to 4 attempts and exponential backoff (60/120/300s) for handling sustained 429 rate limits
Sliding context window trims older exchanges beyond the last 14 to bound token usage and reduce 429 risks
Idea decomposition mechanism splits complex ideas into 2–4 focused sub-tasks, each assigned to the correct project based on repo-specific work, blocking the parent idea until all sub-tasks complete
Sub-tasks are now ordered and can be chained with hard dependencies by setting 'depends_on_previous': true, ensuring backend tasks complete before dependent frontend tasks
Token caps adjusted to reduce API load: bash output capped at 2500 chars, read_file capped at 4000 chars, and max_tokens reduced to 4096 in agent and repair loops

CLAUDE.md from the target repository is fetched and injected into the clarity check and agent system prompt, enabling the bot to answer structural questions autonomously and start with architecture context
Prior Q&A from previous triggers is now injected into the clarity check, ensuring previously resolved questions are not re-asked and improving clarity assessment efficiency

New query_table tool provides read-only access to Azure Table Storage, allowing the agent to inspect live data in ideas, projects, and updates tables to validate assumptions and understand record shapes before writing code
Agent round limit increased from 40 to 60, with a wrap-up nudge injected at round 50 to commit partial work and summarize progress, ensuring no work is lost if the hard cap is reached
Agent now returns a summary explanation when no commits are made, enabling actionable feedback to users and improving transparency in cases where no changes are implemented
Integrated subprocess helper function for posting to the Writing API (quixotry.me), leveraging a pre-warmed token cache for headless operation after one-time human login

dataFlow.label: Decompose complex ideas into sub-tasks if needed · block parent idea
sublines: Evaluate idea complexity · create 2–4 focused sub-tasks
Assign exact project_name per subtask based on repo-specific work
Order sub-tasks and chain dependencies if needed (e.g., backend before frontend)
File sub-tasks via Ideas API · update parent idea status

azure-infrastructure.summary: Defines and provisions all Azure resources at subscription scope using Bicep. Modules cover the Static Web App, Container Registry, Container App Environment (shared Log Analytics workspace), momentum-finder and trail-finder Container Apps, Digits and Dashboard Azure Functions apps, and all RBAC role assignments.
azure-infrastructure.description: Bicep infrastructure-as-code package that defines and provisions every Azure resource in the system at subscription scope. Modules cover each service, the shared networking layer, and all RBAC role assignments.
features: Azure Static Web App for the frontend (my-website)
Container Registry for Docker images (momentum-finder, trail-finder, ideas-bot)
Container App Environment with shared Log Analytics workspace
momentum-finder and trail-finder Container Apps (with scaling rules)
digits, dashboard-api, and ideas-api Azure Functions apps
All Managed Identity RBAC role assignments (Log Analytics Reader, Cost Management Reader)
Container App Job definition for ideas-bot
arch-content-foundry OpenAI resource with GPT-4o deployment (GlobalStandard, 50K TPM)

architecture.overview: Modular Bicep files deployed at subscription scope. Each service has its own module. The Container App Environment module creates the shared Log Analytics workspace used for unified observability across all Container Apps. The arch-content-foundry module provisions an OpenAI resource with a GPT-4o deployment for the arch-content-update CI workflow.
keyPoints: Subscription-scope deployment — all resources land in a single resource group
Managed Identity role assignments are defined alongside the resources that need them
Container Registry uses admin credentials (not Managed Identity) for simplicity
Log Analytics workspace is shared by momentum-finder, trail-finder, and dashboard-api
Scaling rules on Container Apps: min 0 replicas (scale to zero), max 10
arch-content-foundry OpenAI resource uses GlobalStandard SKU with 50K TPM capacity

learning-plan-api.summary: Generates structured learning plans via the Claude API and stores them per user in Azure Table Storage. Google ID tokens are verified server-side on every request, with user isolation enforced by partition key at the storage level.
learning-plan-api.description: Azure Functions app that generates structured learning plans via the Claude API and stores them per user in Azure Table Storage. Google ID tokens are verified server-side for every request.
features: Generates structured learning plans (phases, steps, resources) via Claude API
User authentication via Google OAuth — ID token verified server-side on every request
Stores plans per user in Azure Table Storage (partition key = Google sub claim)
List, create, retrieve, and delete plans per authenticated user
Plan content stored as markdown; frontend renders it with a lightweight parser

architecture.overview: HTTP-triggered Azure Functions backed by Table Storage. Google ID tokens are validated using Google's public key endpoint. The Claude API generates the plan as markdown given a topic, duration, and depth. No session state — fully stateless JWT verification per request.
keyPoints: User isolation enforced at the storage level: partition key = Google sub claim
Google token verification hits accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v3/certs on each request (cached by the SDK)
Claude API called with a structured prompt; plan markdown is stored as-is
Frontend sends the Google ID token in Authorization: Bearer header
No admin interface — plan deletion is user-self-service only

dataFlow.label: User signs in — Google ID token returned to browser
learning-plan-api verifies Google token · reads Table Storage
sublines: returns plans for this user (partition = sub claim)

dataFlow.label: POST /api/plans/generate → Claude API
sublines: structured prompt → markdown learning plan returned

dataFlow.label: POST /api/plans saves plan to Table Storage

posts-api.summary: Azure Functions (Python) API that manages markdown blog posts stored as files in a GitHub repository. Authentication is handled via Google ID tokens; post ownership is enforced per-creator, with an allowlist controlling who can write.
posts-api.description: A serverless write API for quixotry.me's blog. Posts are markdown files with YAML frontmatter committed directly to GitHub; the API sits in front of that storage layer.
features: Create, read, update, and delete blog posts via REST endpoints
Google ID token authentication (Authorization: Bearer header) — unified path for both web app and CLI clients
Support for multiple Google client IDs via comma-separated GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID env var
Per-post ownership: author_email stored in frontmatter; only the creator can edit or delete their posts
Allowlist-gated writes via ALLOWED_WRITERS environment variable
Legacy post support: posts without author_email can be edited by any allowlisted writer; first editor's email is stamped as author
Slug auto-generation from post title using python-slugify
Published/draft state toggled via frontmatter field
Optional description field for drafts; posts can be saved without a description
CLI authentication via Google OAuth InstalledAppFlow with cached ID tokens and updated scope

architecture.overview: Azure Functions v2 app (Python) deployed to a Consumption plan. Each HTTP trigger handles one REST operation. Post storage is GitHub — the API clones/reads/writes markdown files via the GitHub REST API (PyGithub). Auth uses google-auth to validate Google ID tokens server-side; no session state is maintained.
keyPoints: Storage is GitHub, not a database — each post is a .md file with YAML frontmatter in skarumbu/quixotry repo
Google ID token validation via google.oauth2.id_token.verify_oauth2_token against multiple client IDs in GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID env var
ALLOWED_WRITERS env var (comma-separated emails) gates POST; PUT/DELETE further enforce per-post ownership via author_email frontmatter field
No Azure Easy Auth — all auth is application-level; Microsoft identity provider is disabled on the Function App
python-frontmatter used to parse and serialise YAML + markdown body; python-slugify for URL-safe slug generation
Description field is now optional for drafts, allowing posts to be saved without it
CLI uses Google OAuth InstalledAppFlow for authentication, caching ID tokens locally for reuse, with updated scope to https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email

dataFlow.label: Web app / CLI sends request
sublines: Authorization: Bearer <google_id_token> header
JSON body for writes

dataFlow.label: Azure Function validates Google ID token
sublines: google-auth verifies token signature and audience
Returns 401 on failure

dataFlow.label: Ownership / allowlist check
sublines: POST: email must be in ALLOWED_WRITERS
PUT/DELETE: requester must match author_email (or be in allowlist for legacy posts)

dataFlow.label: GitHub API read/write
sublines: PyGithub reads or commits .md files to skarumbu/quixotry
File content is YAML frontmatter + markdown body

dataFlow.label: Response returned to client
sublines: JSON with post data on reads
204 No Content on delete
